natural_occurrence.description: Ammonium carbonates do not occur naturally in significant amounts. Historically, ammonium carbonate was obtained by dry distillation of animal horns and hooves (hence 'hartshorn'), but modern production is entirely synthetic.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting ammonia gas with carbon dioxide and water vapor. E503i (ammonium carbonate) is actually a mixture of ammonium bicarbonate and ammonium carbamate. E503ii (ammonium bicarbonate) is made by passing CO2 through aqueous ammonia solution.

other_uses.cosmetics: Rarely used; primarily in hair dye products as alkalizing agent.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Expectorant, smelling salts (aromatic spirits of ammonia).

eu.conditions: Restricted to use in certain food categories, primarily baked goods. Decomposes completely during baking, leaving no residue.

usa.notes: Limited to baked goods where it fully decomposes during baking.

japan.notes: Primarily used in traditional confections and crackers.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (acceptable for use in baked goods)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no residue remains in finished product)

commonly_found_in: Traditional European cookies and crackers (especially Scandinavian)
Flat, crispy baked goods (like biscotti and gingersnaps)
fun_facts: Known as 'baker's ammonia' - used in baking long before baking soda became common (since the Middle Ages)
E503i is the active ingredient in old-fashioned SMELLING SALTS used to revive people who have fainted
Completely decomposes into ammonia, CO2, and water vapor during baking - leaving zero residue in the final product
The ammonia smell disappears entirely during baking; if you can still smell it, the product wasn't baked long enough
Produces exceptionally crispy, airy textures that baking soda or baking powder cannot replicate
Historically called 'hartshorn' because it was originally made by heating deer antlers
Still preferred by professional bakers for certain traditional recipes despite the initial ammonia smell
